# TIA Risk Stratification and Stroke Mechanisms

TIAs: As a general rule of thumb, all TIA patients should be observed for 24 hours and evaluated expeditiously for high- risk stroke mechanism. If they want to go home, tell them the risk: The ABCD2 score predicts risk of stroke within 2 days of a TIA (Lancet 369: 282-292, 2007): Age ‚Č•60 years [1 point] Blood pressure ‚Č•140/90 mm Hg [1 point] Clinical features: unilateral weakness [2 points], speech impairment without weakness [1 point] Duration ‚Č•60 min [2 points] or 10‚Äď59 min [1 point] Diabetes [1 point] Score 6‚Äď7 = High risk = 8.1% 2-day risk 4‚Äď5 = Moderate risk = 4.1% 0‚Äď3 = Low risk = 1.0%

Stroke mechanisms: Most common mechanisms of stroke include:
- Cardioembolism (~20% ischemic stroke): Atrial fibrillation (most common, esp in older adults), CHF/CM with low EF, LV or LAA thrombus, cardiac tumor (myxoma/fibroelastoma), mechanical valve, endocarditis (infectious or marantic [autoimmune/malignancy]), paradoxical embolism (PFO, pulm AVM)
- Large-artery atherosclerosis (~15%) ‚Äď extracranial or intracranial (affects treatment choice)
- Small-vessel disease (~25%) ‚Äď must be subcortical/brainstem/cerebellum. Small cortical strokes are generally embolic. Lacunar strokes are usually linked to vascular risk factors, esp uncontrolled DM and tobacco
- Rare causes (<5%) ‚Äď see below, also consider hypercoagulable state from undiagnosed malignancy
- Cryptogenic stroke (~20-30%) ‚Äď no cause found despite thorough workup. If embolic-appearing, may be ESUS (embolic stroke of undetermined source), consider long-term Afib monitoring, no role for empiric AC per ESUS trials to date.

Young Stroke: In addition to conventional stroke mechanisms, consider the following for patients <60 years old: Cardiac disease (including congenital, rheumatic valve disease, mitral valve prolapse, patent foramen ovale, endocarditis, atrial myxoma, arrhythmias, cardiac surgery) Large vessel disease Ôāß Premature atherosclerosis ‚Äď consider radiation vasculopathy in patients with previous head/neck irradiation. Also consider HIV Ôāß Dissection (spontaneous or traumatic) Ôāß Inherited metabolic diseases (homocystinuria, Fabry‚Äôs, pseudoxanthoma elasticum, MELAS syndrome) Ôāß Fibromuscular dysplasia Ôāß Infection (bacterial, fungal, tuberculosis, syphilis, Lyme) Ôāß Vasculitis (collagen vascular diseases ‚ÄĒ systemic lupus erythematosus, rheumatoid arthritis, Sj√∂gren‚Äôs syndrome, polyarteritis nodosa; Takayasu‚Äôs disease, Wegener‚Äôs syndrome, cryoglobulinemia, sarcoidosis, inflammatory bowel disease, isolated central nervous system angiitis) Ôāß Moyamoya disease Ôāß Toxic (illicit drugs ‚ÄĒ cocaine, heroin, phencyclidine; therapeutic drugs ‚ÄĒ L-asparaginase, cytosine arabinoside) Small vessel disease Ôāß Vasculopathy (infectious, noninfectious, microangiopathy) Hematologic disease Ôāß Sickle-cell disease, leukemia, thrombocytosis, polycythemia vera Ôāß Hypercoagulable states (antiphospholipid antibody syndromes, deficiency of antithrombin III or protein S or C, resistance to activated protein C, increased factor VIII, MTHFR) Ôāß DIC, TTP Ôāß Venous occlusion (dehydration, parameningeal infection, meningitis, neoplasm, polycythemia, leukemia, inflammatory bowel disease)
